title: Coarse hyperbolicity and closed orbits for quasigeodesic flows
speaker: Steven Frankel
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Pseudo-Anosov benchmark and classical closing
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets the model case of pseudo-Anosov flows on hyperbolic 3-manifolds, explaining stable/unstable transverse hyperbolicity, Thurston-norm motivation, and the Anosov closing lemma as the template for producing closed orbits.

proof_stages.label: Quasigeodesic-flow setup and target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Quasigeodesic flows are defined by lifted orbit lines being uniform quasigeodesics in hyperbolic space, their standard sources and Thurston-norm connections are reviewed, and Calegari's closed-orbit conjecture is stated as the main theorem.

proof_stages.label: Flow space and endpoint decompositions
proof_stages.summary: The lifted flow is collapsed to its Hausdorff orbit plane, equipped with positive and negative endpoint maps to the sphere at infinity, whose point-preimage components form unbounded decompositions with compact pairwise intersections.

proof_stages.label: Universal-circle compactification by ends
proof_stages.summary: Using Freudenthal ends of the positive and negative decomposition leaves, the flow plane is compactified functorially to a closed disk, so the deck-group action extends to the boundary circle, the universal circle.

proof_stages.label: Circle action detects large-scale flow dynamics
proof_stages.summary: The universal-circle action is shown to detect closed orbits through fixed points in the flow disk, while the endpoint maps extend to the compactified disk and agree on the universal circle to give an equivariant sphere-filling boundary map.

proof_stages.label: Homotopy closing via linked leaves
proof_stages.summary: The coarse contraction/expansion encoded by the decompositions and the universal circle is formulated as a homotopy closing lemma: recurrent orbits with linked positive and negative leaves yield fundamental-group elements eventually represented by closed orbits, and planar topology supplies such linked configurations.

proof_stages.label: Straightening toward a pseudo-Anosov model
proof_stages.summary: For the broader deformation conjecture, the decomposition data on the universal circle are straightened into singular foliations on a new plane, a corresponding group action and flow are reconstructed, and the boundary decomposition is used to argue that the resulting flow remains quasigeodesic.

examples.name: Suspension flow of a pseudo-Anosov surface homeomorphism
examples.note: The mapping-torus suspension makes the stable/unstable foliations and quasigeodesicity concrete, and later reappears as the classical Cannon-Thurston-type case of the boundary map.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the suspension flow from a surface mapping torus, identifies its stable/unstable leaves, and later sketches why such suspension flows are quasigeodesic via the cyclic-cover argument

passes.reason: standard mapping-torus suspension used as a canonical source of pseudo-Anosov/quasigeodesic flows

examples.name: Weeks manifold obstruction
examples.note: The Weeks manifold is used as a negative illustration: because its group has no suitable circle action, it admits no pseudo-Anosov flow.
examples.engagement_reason: the Weeks manifold is invoked only as an example ruled out by the universal-circle obstruction, with no computation or verification of the obstruction on it

passes.reason: the Weeks manifold is the minimum-volume closed hyperbolic 3-manifold and is used as an obstruction showing some closed hyperbolic 3-manifolds admit no pseudo-Anosov flows
the Weeks manifold is the minimum-volume closed orientable hyperbolic 3-manifold and is used as an obstruction showing that not every such manifold admits pseudo-Anosov flows
The Weeks manifold is the minimal-volume closed orientable hyperbolic 3-manifold and is invoked as an obstruction showing that some manifolds admit no pseudo-Anosov flow.

examples.name: Dilatation near the boundary of a fibered face
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker recalls that pseudo-Anosov monodromy dilatations blow up toward the boundary of a fibered face, illustrating why transverse representatives relative to a flow encode useful dynamics.
examples.engagement_reason: dilatation near the boundary of a fibered face is discussed as a known phenomenon one should see geometrically, but no concrete calculation or derivation is carried out

passes.reason: approaching the boundary of a fibered face is a limiting/boundary regime used to illustrate dilatation blow-up, not to refute a claim
